text: **Why it's better:** Tense shift turns the refrain from a loop into a resolution. Every "I began again" before it sounds like restarting the same cycle. "I have begun" breaks the cycle — three words that rewrite every prior instance. The Vonnegut mechanic at its highest level: the refrain changes meaning without (almost) changing words. Sean's version has direction. Vonnegut's is circular. Sean's escapes.

### Domestic Observer Mode — Applied > For nine years I commuted two hours each way to organize media files in the windowless basement of a building so old it smelled like it was working toward its second millennium. New York Life Insurance Company. The name alone should've been the tell — I was insuring someone else's life while mine collected dust between hard drives and hollow water cooler conversations. I smiled through them like a man who'd been smiling so long his face forgot it was a choice. > > I had three animated shorts, five scripts, and thousands

of dollars distributed across the pockets of people who swore they could get my work in front of the right people. The right people were apparently busy. Screenwriting was supposed to be the exit, but the more I learned about Hollywood, the more it sounded like the same basement with better lighting. Writers rooms had shrunk to fit nepo babies and the friends of friends of friends, and the golden age of the evergreen sitcom had packed its bags sometime around 2015 without telling anyone where it was going. > > Then one Tuesday in March — I remember it

was a Tuesday because the bathroom stalls at New York Life were marginally less repulsive on Tuesdays — I was scrolling Instagram while perched on a toilet older than my freelance career, and I saw it. People were posting pictures of themselves in the style of Studio Ghibli. ChatGPT had learned to draw. I'd seen the Will Smith spaghetti video. I was a non-believer. But sitting there, something shifted. It was the kind of epiphany you'd expect to happen on a mountaintop or in a therapist's office, not in a bathroom stall that smelled like it was staging an intervention

### Gonzo Technical Mode — Applied > January 1st, 2024. High on mushrooms, staring at myself in a bathroom mirror at — I want to say 2 AM but time had stopped being reliable about three hours earlier. I made a declaration to the stranger in the reflection: this is the year I begin again. The stranger did not look convinced. > > By 7:15 the next morning, I was slowly migrating onto the Staten Island ferry with the rest of the lost souls stuck in the same pattern. Our baggy, bloodshot eyes staring into the void as we collectively

migrate towards the ship of the damned. Sheep heading to the slaughterhouse we call our office. New year. New resolutions. New ways to trick ourselves into happiness. The hamster wheel rolls on. > > The escape plan was screenwriting. I had five scripts, three animated shorts, and a growing portfolio of receipts from consultants, coverage services, and festival submission fees — $4,200 across 2022 and 2023 alone, paid to people whose primary skill was maintaining eye contact while saying "it's all about getting it in front of the right people." The right people were apparently on a different planet. Meanwhile,

Hollywood's writers went on strike over AI, which I found amusing in the way you find something amusing right before it becomes your entire life. I'd seen the Will Smith spaghetti video. Computers couldn't do what humans do. > > MARCH 25TH, 2025. New York Life bathroom stall. Instagram open. Studio Ghibli portraits flooding every feed. ChatGPT-4o image generation had dropped and the internet lost its collective mind. I was sitting on a toilet that had seen more human despair than most therapists, scrolling through AI-generated art with the quiet horror of a man who had just bet his career

### Beat Flow Mode — Applied > I was high on mushrooms staring at my own face in the mirror — the face of a man who had been commuting to the same windowless basement for nine years and organizing the same media files in the same dead air of a building so old it had given up pretending to smell like anything other than what it was — and I said to myself, to the stranger behind the glass with the bloodshot eyes and the jaw clenched tight like a fist that forgot how to open, I said this

is the year, this is the one, and the stranger just stared back like he'd heard it all before because he had. > > And the ferry the next morning — the Staten Island ferry packed with the same lost souls shuffling aboard in their winter coats and their dead eyes and their travel mugs filled with caffeine and hair of the dog, an elixir of motivation and a way to cope — all of us migrating across that gray water toward Manhattan like a slow parade of the professionally damned, and I remember thinking even then, even in the

middle of the resolution and the mushroom afterglow and the desperate wanting, I remember thinking this feels exactly like last year. > > Nine years of fluorescent light and lanyard swipes and water cooler small talk about nothing — nine years of animated shorts nobody watched and scripts nobody read and thousands of dollars hemorrhaged into the pockets of consultants who promised the right people and delivered the same empty room — nine years of screenwriting podcasts and festival submission fees and the slow erosion of believing your own talent means anything when the door won't open — and then

one morning in March, sitting in a bathroom stall at New York Life Insurance Company, scrolling Instagram on a toilet older than my ambition, I saw the Studio Ghibli portraits flooding every feed and felt something crack open in my chest like a door I didn't know was there — and I walked through it and I kept walking and I never turned around. > > That was then. Now I wake up before my alarm. Fuelled by the new found skills that didn't exist 18 months ago, fresh brewed coffee, and for once in my life, a glimmer of

### Minimalist Absurdist Mode — Applied > Here is what happened. I organized media files in a basement for nine years. The building was 150 years old. It smelled like it. I commuted two hours each way on the Staten Island ferry. So did everyone else. We did not discuss this. > > I had three animated shorts and five screenplays and several thousand dollars' worth of advice from people who knew the right people. The right people never materialized. I began again. > > In 2024 I saw the Will Smith spaghetti video and decided AI was not a

threat. Writers went on strike over it. I found this amusing. I began again. > > In March 2025 I was sitting on a toilet at New York Life Insurance Company scrolling Instagram. Everyone was a Studio Ghibli character. ChatGPT had learned to draw. I had not learned to draw in nine years of art school. The machine learned in an afternoon. I began again. > > I replaced screenwriting podcasts with AI tutorials. I replaced writing exercises with vibecoding. I learned a phrase called "Product Management." By November I had a job doing it. I was thirty-three years old.

# Why Every Product Manager Should Learn Python

In today's data-driven product landscape, the line between technical and non-technical roles is blurring fast. Product managers who once relied solely on engineers for every data pull or quick analysis are finding themselves at a disadvantage. Enter Python—the programming language that's quietly becoming a PM superpower.

You don't need to become a software engineer to benefit from Python. Even basic proficiency unlocks the ability to analyze user behavior data, automate tedious reporting tasks, prototype ideas, and have more substantive conversations with your engineering team. Imagine pulling your own metrics from a database at 11 PM before a big meeting, or running a quick analysis on customer feedback without waiting three days for the data team's queue to clear.

Beyond the practical wins, learning Python builds something more valuable: technical empathy. You'll understand why certain features take longer to build, what's actually feasible in a sprint, and how to scope requirements that engineers can actually execute on. In an era where AI tools and data fluency define competitive advantage, PMs who can code—even just a little—are positioning themselves as indispensable. In this post, we'll explore exactly why Python is worth your time and where to start.

# The AI-Native Era Has Arrived

Something has shifted in 2026, and it's impossible to ignore. The startup landscape isn't just being influenced by AI anymore—it's being rebuilt from the ground up by it. We've entered the era of AI-native startups: companies designed from day one around large language models, autonomous agents, and machine learning pipelines as core infrastructure, not bolted-on features.

Unlike the "AI-powered" wave of 2023 and 2024, today's AI-native founders aren't asking *how do we add AI to our product?* They're asking *what becomes possible when AI is the product?* The result is a new generation of lean, hyper-efficient companies—often three-person teams generating revenue that once required staffs of fifty. Vertical agents are replacing entire SaaS categories. Solo founders are launching profitable businesses in weeks. And venture capital is racing to keep up with valuations that defy traditional benchmarks.

But this transformation isn't just about speed or scale. It's reshaping how we think about work, ownership, defensibility, and what it even means to build a company. In this post, we'll explore the forces driving the rise of AI-native startups, the patterns separating winners from hype, and what comes

# Choosing Between Claude and GPT for Product Work

If you're building anything with AI right now, you've probably stared at the same decision: Claude or GPT? It's tempting to treat this like picking a database or a cloud provider—run a benchmark, pick a winner, move on. But that framing misses what actually matters when these models become part of your product.

The truth is, Claude and GPT have meaningfully different personalities, strengths, and failure modes. Claude tends to excel at long-context reasoning, careful writing, and following nuanced instructions without going off the rails. GPT often shines in raw versatility, tool use, and a sprawling ecosystem of integrations. But "excel" and "shine" depend entirely on what you're building, who's using it, and what trade-offs you can live with.

In this post, we'll move past the leaderboard wars and look at the decision the way a product team actually makes it: by examining real use cases, latency and cost realities, output quality on the tasks you care about, and the often-overlooked factors like API stability and how each model handles edge cases your users will inevitably find. By the end, you'll have a clearer framework for picking the right model—or knowing when you need both.

# The Future of Crypto Product Management: Building for a Decentralized Tomorrow

Crypto product management is no longer a niche discipline—it's rapidly becoming one of the most demanding and rewarding roles in tech. As blockchain technology matures and adoption accelerates, the role of a crypto PM is evolving far beyond traditional product frameworks. Today's crypto product managers must balance user experience with on-chain mechanics, navigate shifting regulatory landscapes, and design for communities rather than just customers.

The future will demand a new breed of PM—one fluent in tokenomics, governance models, and smart contract limitations, while still grounded in the fundamentals of solving real user problems. We're moving past the speculative era and into a phase where utility, interoperability, and seamless Web2-to-Web3 experiences will define winning products. AI integration, account abstraction, and real-world asset tokenization are reshaping what's possible, while decentralized decision-making is challenging how product roadmaps get built in the first place.

For PMs entering or growing in this space, the opportunity has never been greater—or more complex. In this post, we'll explore the key trends shaping crypto product management, the skills tomorrow's leaders will need,

# Why Every PM Needs to Understand LLMs

If you're a product manager in 2025 and you're still treating large language models as someone else's problem, you're already behind. LLMs aren't just another tech trend to delegate to your engineering team—they're rapidly becoming foundational infrastructure, much like databases or APIs were in previous eras.

Here's the uncomfortable truth: your competitors are shipping AI-powered features weekly. Your users are increasingly comparing your product not just to direct alternatives, but to the seamless, conversational experiences they get from tools like ChatGPT and Claude. The bar has shifted, and "we'll add AI later" is no longer a viable strategy.

But understanding LLMs goes beyond knowing how to write a prompt. As a PM, you need to grasp the trade-offs between latency and quality, the realities of hallucinations, the economics of token-based pricing, and the unique UX challenges of probabilistic interfaces. You need to know when an LLM is the right tool—and crucially, when it isn't.

In this post, we'll break down the core LLM concepts every PM should master, the questions you should be asking your engineering team, and how to evaluate whether AI features will actually move your metrics.

# Hiring Your First AI Engineer: A Startup's Make-or-Break Moment

Bringing on your first AI engineer isn't just another hire—it's a defining decision that will shape your startup's technical trajectory for years to come. Unlike adding a fifth backend developer to an established team, this person will set the standards, choose the tools, and architect the systems that everyone after them will build upon.

The stakes are higher than most founders realize. AI engineering sits at the intersection of software development, machine learning, data infrastructure, and product intuition. Hire someone too academic, and you'll get beautiful models that never ship. Hire someone too engineering-focused, and you might miss the nuances that separate a working prototype from a production-grade AI system. Hire someone chasing hype, and you'll burn through runway on technologies that don't fit your actual problem.

Yet getting it right can be transformative. The right first AI engineer doesn't just build features—they identify which problems are worth solving with AI, push back on unrealistic expectations, and create the foundation that lets your team move fast without breaking things (too often).

In this post, we'll break down what to look for, what to avoid, and how to structure the role for long-term success.

# Why Prompt Engineering Belongs in Every Product Manager's Toolkit

If you're a product manager in 2024, you've probably used ChatGPT to brainstorm features, summarize user interviews, or draft a PRD. But here's the uncomfortable truth: most PMs are leaving 80% of AI's value on the table simply because they don't know how to ask the right way.

Prompt engineering isn't just a buzzword for developers and AI researchers—it's quickly becoming a core competency for product managers. The difference between a vague request and a well-crafted prompt can mean the difference between generic fluff and a strategic competitive analysis you can actually use in your next stakeholder meeting.

Think about it: PMs already excel at translating ambiguous problems into clear requirements, aligning cross-functional teams around shared context, and iterating based on feedback. These are the exact same skills that make someone great at prompting AI. You're essentially managing a brilliant but literal-minded teammate who needs precise direction to deliver great work.

In this post, we'll break down practical prompt engineering frameworks tailored specifically for PM workflows—from writing user stories to analyzing customer feedback at scale. By the end, you'll have a rep

# Measuring AI Feature Success: Why Traditional Metrics Aren't Enough

Launching an AI feature feels a lot like sending a probe into space. You can track whether it left the launchpad, but understanding whether it's actually delivering value? That's where things get complicated.

Most product teams default to familiar metrics: adoption rates, click-throughs, and time-on-task. While these numbers tell part of the story, they often miss what really matters with AI-powered experiences. A user might click your AI suggestion button repeatedly—but are they doing it because it's helpful, or because they're frustrated and trying again?

Measuring AI feature success requires a different lens. Unlike deterministic features where success is binary, AI experiences exist on a spectrum of quality, trust, and perceived intelligence. Users don't just need features that work; they need features they can rely on, understand, and integrate into their workflows.

In this post, we'll explore the metrics that actually matter when evaluating AI features with real users—from trust calibration and task completion quality to the subtle behavioral signals that reveal whether your AI is genuinely augmenting users or quietly creating friction. Because shipping an AI feature is just the beginning; knowing if it's truly succe

# Rolling Out a New AI Tool? Here's Where Most Teams Go Wrong

Introducing a new AI tool to your team should feel like an upgrade—a chance to automate the tedious, accelerate the complex, and free up everyone's time for higher-value work. In reality, it often feels more like herding cats through a software demo. Some team members dive in headfirst, others quietly resist, and a few keep asking why the old way wasn't good enough.

The truth is, onboarding an AI tool isn't just a technical rollout. It's a change management challenge wrapped in a software implementation. The most powerful platform in the world won't deliver results if your team doesn't trust it, understand it, or know when to actually use it. And with AI tools evolving faster than most onboarding playbooks can keep up, leaders need a smarter, more human approach to adoption.

In this post, we'll walk through a practical framework for introducing AI tools to your team—one that addresses skepticism, builds confidence, and turns early adopters into internal champions. Whether you're rolling out a writing assistant, a data analysis platform, or a customer service bot, these principles will help your team move from cautious curiosity to confident, everyday use.

# Fine-Tuning vs. Prompting: Choosing the Right Path for Your LLM Project

When building applications with large language models, one of the first architectural decisions you'll face is deceptively simple: should you fine-tune the model or rely on clever prompting? The answer, like most things in machine learning, is "it depends"—and getting it wrong can cost you significant time, money, and performance.

Prompting offers speed and flexibility. You can iterate in minutes, swap models easily, and avoid the infrastructure overhead of training pipelines. But as your prompts grow longer to handle edge cases, you'll hit context window limits, rising inference costs, and inconsistent outputs that no amount of "please respond in JSON" can fully tame.

Fine-tuning, on the other hand, bakes behavior directly into the model's weights. The result is shorter prompts, faster inference, and more reliable outputs for specialized tasks. The catch? You'll need quality training data, compute resources, and a willingness to retrain whenever requirements shift or base models improve.

In this post, we'll break down the real-world trade-offs between these two approaches—covering cost, latency, maintainability, and accuracy—so you can make an informed choice for your next

# The Quiet Revolution in PM Work: How Model Context Protocols Are Changing Everything

If you've spent the last year watching AI tools pile onto your product manager tech stack, you're not alone—and you're probably exhausted. Every week brings another assistant promising to write your PRDs, summarize your user interviews, or draft your roadmap. But most of them live in isolated silos, disconnected from the actual context of your work.

That's starting to change. Model Context Protocols (MCPs) are emerging as the connective tissue that lets AI assistants tap directly into the tools PMs already rely on—Jira, Linear, Figma, Notion, Slack, analytics platforms, and customer feedback systems. Instead of copy-pasting context into a chat window, your AI can pull live data, understand current sprint priorities, and reference real customer quotes without you lifting a finger.

For product managers, this shift is more than a productivity bump. It's a fundamental rewiring of how we synthesize information, make decisions, and communicate across teams. The PMs who learn to design effective context pipelines—not just clever prompts—will pull ahead. In this post, we'll break down what MCPs actually do, why they matter for product work specifically, and

# The Hidden Costs of AI Infrastructure: What's Really Powering the Revolution

When companies tout their latest AI breakthroughs, the conversation usually centers on capabilities—faster responses, smarter outputs, more human-like interactions. What rarely makes the headlines? The staggering, often invisible costs propping up these innovations.

Behind every chatbot query and generated image lies a sprawling network of GPU clusters, cooling systems, and data centers consuming electricity at rates that rival small nations. Training a single large language model can cost millions of dollars and emit hundreds of tons of carbon dioxide. But the expenses don't stop at training. Inference—the day-to-day running of AI models—quietly drains budgets and resources at scale, with each user interaction adding to a growing operational tab.

Then there are the costs few discuss: water consumption for cooling, rare earth minerals for hardware, specialized talent commanding premium salaries, and the mounting technical debt of maintaining ever-more-complex systems. Add regulatory compliance, security infrastructure, and the environmental toll of hardware obsolescence, and the true price tag of AI becomes dizzying.

# The Rise of Agentic Workflows in Modern SaaS

For years, SaaS products have promised to make us more productive—offering dashboards, automations, and integrations that streamline our daily tasks. But a quiet revolution is reshaping the landscape: **agentic workflows**. Unlike traditional automation, which follows rigid, predefined rules, agentic workflows leverage AI agents capable of reasoning, planning, and executing multi-step tasks with minimal human intervention.

Think of the difference between a calculator and a financial advisor. One waits for your input; the other understands your goals, gathers information, makes decisions, and takes action on your behalf. That's the leap modern SaaS is making. Tools like sales platforms, customer support suites, and project management systems are embedding intelligent agents that can draft emails, qualify leads, resolve tickets, and even orchestrate cross-functional processes—autonomously.

What makes this shift transformative isn't just the technology, but the *operating model* it enables. Teams are no longer the bottleneck for repetitive cognitive work. Instead, humans become orchestrators, setting objectives while agents handle execution. In this post, we'll explore how agentic workflows are redefining product

# AI Features vs. AI Products: Why the Distinction Matters

Every company is racing to "add AI" to their offerings, but there's a critical difference between bolting an AI feature onto an existing product and building a true AI product. Confusing the two leads to disappointing launches, wasted engineering cycles, and customers who shrug at your shiny new chatbot.

An **AI feature** enhances an existing workflow. Think of Gmail's Smart Compose or Notion's AI writing assistant—useful additions that make established tools incrementally better. The core value proposition existed before AI showed up; the model just sands down friction or speeds things up.

An **AI product**, by contrast, couldn't exist without AI at its center. Remove the model and the entire experience collapses. ChatGPT, Midjourney, and Cursor aren't products with AI sprinkled on top—they're products whose fundamental utility is generated, decision by decision, by the model itself. The interface, pricing, and user expectations all revolve around what the AI can do.

This distinction shapes everything: how you design, how you handle errors, how you price, and how you measure success. In this post, we'll explore why teams keep blurring this line—and what it costs them when they do.

# The PM Role Is Being Rewritten in Real Time

Walk into any product org at a Fortune 500 today and you'll notice something different. The product manager who used to spend Monday mornings wrangling Jira tickets, drafting PRDs, and chasing down stakeholder feedback is now doing something else entirely—or at least, doing those things in a fraction of the time.

AI isn't just another tool in the PM stack. It's quietly dismantling the traditional definition of the role. Tasks that once consumed 60% of a PM's week—synthesizing user research, writing specs, analyzing funnel data, drafting release notes—can now be handled in minutes by a well-prompted LLM. What's left is the work that actually moves the needle: judgment, prioritization, cross-functional alignment, and a deeper understanding of what customers truly need.

At large companies, where PMs have historically been buried under coordination overhead, the shift is even more dramatic. Senior leaders are starting to ask harder questions: Do we need ten PMs on this product, or three exceptional ones with AI leverage? What does career progression look like when entry-level tasks evaporate?
